original_abstract:
The mechanism by which copper-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions cleave carbon-halogen bonds has long been a subject of debate, largely due to the transient nature of high-valent copper intermediates and the complex redox pathways available to the metal. We report the synthesis and full characterization of stable alkyl Cu(III) complexes generated through the oxidative addition of $\alpha$-haloacetonitriles to both ionic and neutral Cu(I) precursors. This rare stability is achieved by employing strong-field trifluoromethyl ligands, which increase the kinetic barrier for reductive elimination. 

Mechanistic investigations, supported by kinetic analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, reveal that the reaction pathway is highly dependent on the nature of the copper precursor. While the ionic cuprate reacts primarily via an $S_N2$-type substitution, the neutral bipyridine-ligated complex undergoes oxidative addition through a halogen-atom transfer (XAT) mechanism. Notably, we observed significant ligand acceleration in the neutral system, mirroring the reactivity patterns seen in practical copper-catalyzed couplings of alkyl electrophiles with various nucleophiles. These findings provide direct evidence for a Cu(I)/Cu(III) redox manifold in alkyl cross-couplings and offer a framework for the rational design of more efficient copper catalysts.
humanized_abstract:
The mechanism of cleavage of carbon–halogen bonds in copper-catalysed cross-coupling reactions has been the subject of much speculation over the years due to the instability of the proposed high-valent copper species and the ambiguities inherent in the redox chemistry of copper. Here we present a detailed investigation into the preparation and structural elucidation of stable alkyl Cu(III) species obtained by oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitriles to Cu(I) salts, both ionic and neutral. Stable alkyl Cu(III) species are made stable by coordination of strong-field ligands (trifluoromethyl) which hinder reductive elimination.

We recently communicated1 that cuprate reactivity can be controlled to undergo $S_N2$-type $trans$-effect assisted $S_N2$ substitution vs oxidative addition involving a halogen atom transfer (XAT) mechanism by changing the copper source from ionic to neutral neutral with a ligand environment that includes two five-membered chelating rings, i.e. bipyridine (2,2'-bipy). The neutral complex exhibits substantial ligand acceleration, as seen with other combinations of alkyl electrophiles and nucleophiles with copper catalysts. With these findings at hand, we are in a position to confirm the Cu(I)/Cu(III) redox manifolds in cross coupling reactions of alkyl electrophiles and to learn how to create high reactivity at the metal center.

We recently communicated1 that cuprate reactivity can be controlled to undergo $S_N2$-type $trans$-effect assisted $S_N2$ substitution vs oxidative addition involving a halogen atom transfer (XAT) mechanism by changing the copper source from ionic to neutral neutral with a ligand environment that includes two five-membered chelating rings, i.e. bipyridine (2,2'-bipy). document.input:
The mechanism by which copper-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions cleave carbon-halogen bonds has long been a subject of debate, largely due to the transient nature of high-valent copper intermediates and the complex redox pathways available to the metal.
